title: Caso de Negócio
text:
This Business Case justifies the investment in an AI-based pipeline for automated screening of subsea imagery, with the primary goal of strengthening ecological understanding and environmental governance in Petrobras Subsea operations.
The initiative responds to a critical scalability challenge: the exponential growth of AUV inspection data has outpaced the capacity for manual ecological analysis, increasing the risk of delayed assessments, specialist overload, and potential loss of environmentally relevant information.
By introducing AI-assisted screening as an enabling technology, the project delivers environmental value first, while also generating measurable operational and economic benefits.







AUV inspection campaigns generate millions of images per field
Ecological and environmental analysis relies heavily on manual expert screening
Increased data volume leads to:

Longer analysis cycles
Higher specialist workload
Reduced scalability of environmental studies

Environmental confidence depends on human capacity, which does not scale linearly

This creates a structural risk for:

Environmental baseline studies
Monitoring and impact assessments
Support to licensing and regulatory processes





If no action is taken:

Ecologically relevant benthic data may be delayed, overlooked, or underutilized
Environmental assessments become bottlenecks in subsea operations
Specialist overload increases operational and human risk
Environmental intelligence remains fragmented and non-reusable

This risk directly impacts Petrobras’ ability to operate responsibly and sustainably in complex offshore environments.







The proposed solution introduces AI-based automated screening of subsea imagery, acting as a scaling mechanism for ecological intelligence.
The system does not replace experts; it filters noise so that experts can focus on ecologically relevant content with higher confidence and consistency.




The initiative aligns with Petrobras Subsea strategic drivers:

Environmental stewardship and ecological responsibility
Digitalization of subsea inspection and monitoring
Reduction of human exposure and specialist overload
Creation of reusable digital environmental assets
Long-term cost efficiency through scalability









Improved identification of benthic-relevant imagery at scale
Higher confidence in environmental baseline studies
Reduced risk of missing ecologically relevant features
More consistent ecological interpretation across campaigns and fields






Reduction in manual screening effort (≥ 30%)
Shorter analysis cycles (≥ 25%)
Lower cost per inspection campaign
Better allocation of specialist time to high-value analysis






Creation of a reusable environmental AI capability
Accumulation of curated, versioned ecological datasets
Foundation for future environmental intelligence initiatives
Progressive adoption of MLOps within SUB









Data volume growth is accelerating
Manual processes are reaching practical limits
AI/CV technologies are sufficiently mature for screening tasks
Delaying action increases ecological and operational risk






Reduces upfront investment and risk
Allows early validation with real operational data
Enables iterative improvement with SME oversight
Preserves flexibility for future expansion









Data science and engineering effort
Compute and storage resources
SME validation and annotation effort
Governance, documentation, and maintenance






Reduced specialist hours per campaign
Faster environmental assessments
Reuse of models and datasets across fields
Avoided cost of delayed or repeated analysis





ROI is driven not only by cost reduction, but by risk avoidance and value preservation, particularly:

Avoidance of ecological oversight
Avoidance of rework due to late detection
Preservation of environmental credibility














Option
Description
Assessment




Do Nothing
Maintain manual screening
Not scalable, high risk


Partial Automation
Rule-based or limited tools
Low robustness


AI-Based Screening (Proposed)
Precision-first AI + SME validation
Best balance of value and risk















Risk
Impact
Mitigation




Model underperformance
Reduced trust
Conservative precision-first strategy


Annotation subjectivity
Inconsistent outputs
Guidelines + consensus


Adoption resistance
Low usage
SME involvement from start


Over-automation
Ecological risk
Human-in-the-loop governance








≥ 30% reduction in manual screening effort
≥ 25% reduction in analysis cycle time
≥ 85–90% retention of true benthic data
Positive validation by NOAA / SUB environmental specialists





Proceed with the proposed MVP for AI-based automated screening of subsea imagery, recognizing it as a strategic environmental investment, with automation serving as an enabler for ecological excellence, not as an end in itself.
Approval of this Business Case authorizes transition to detailed Planning and controlled Execution phases.

title: Scope Statement
text:
This Project Scope Statement formally defines the project scope baseline at a narrative level, specifying:

What is included in the project
What is explicitly excluded
The boundaries of the MVP
Key deliverables and acceptance criteria
Constraints and assumptions that shape execution

This document is a mandatory PMBOK artifact and, together with the WBS and WBS Dictionary, forms the Scope Baseline of the project.




The project will design, develop, and validate an AI-enabled environmental intelligence pipeline to support automated screening of AUV imagery, with the primary objective of protecting and scaling ecological analysis of benthic environments.
The solution will apply Computer Vision techniques to classify still images as:

Benthic-relevant (ecologically significant)
Non-benthic / non-ecologically relevant

Automation is introduced strictly as an enabler, with human-in-the-loop validation maintained as a mandatory control to preserve ecological confidence.







Automated screening of AUV still images using AI/CV
Batch, offline-first processing of inspection datasets
AI-assisted annotation workflows
Precision-oriented binary classification
Ecological validation and acceptance by environmental SMEs
Versioned datasets and model artifacts
Traceability and audit support






Initial validation in at least one field (Viola or Espadarte)
Demonstrated generalization across two fields
Use in environmental baseline studies and inspection support














Deliverable
Description
Acceptance Authority




AI Screening Model
Binary CV model for benthic relevance
Environmental SME Lead


Inference Pipeline
Offline batch screening capability
Data Science Lead


Annotation Guidelines
Ecological labeling rules
Environmental SMEs


Validation Reports
Performance and ecological assurance
Sponsor / NOAA


Versioned Datasets
Curated, auditable datasets
Data Governance


Project Documentation
Governance and technical artifacts
Project Sponsor







The project deliverables will be accepted when:

Ecological Data Protection Index (KPI‑3) meets or exceeds agreed threshold (≥ 85–90%)
Manual screening effort is reduced by ≥ 30% without ecological degradation
Environmental screening lead time is reduced by ≥ 25%
Environmental SMEs validate usability and confidence
Assets are versioned, auditable, and reusable





The following are explicitly excluded from this project scope:

Real-time or near-real-time processing
Full taxonomic classification of organisms
Autonomous decision-making without human validation
Advanced multimodal fusion (sonar, bathymetry) beyond preparatory design
AUV operations, mission planning, or data acquisition
External data sharing or publication workflows






No pre-labeled dataset available at project start
Natural class imbalance in subsea imagery
Offline-first processing requirement
Environmental variability (turbidity, lighting, backscatter)
Use of license-compliant AI frameworks only
Availability of environmental SMEs for validation






Still imagery is sufficient for MVP validation
Environmental SMEs will be available for iterative validation
Compute resources are available to support batch processing
Binary classification provides sufficient early value
MVP results will inform future expansion decisions






Any change to this Scope Statement requires a formal Change Request
Changes must be evaluated for:

Ecological risk
Impact on OKRs and KPIs
Schedule and cost implications

Scope changes are subject to Sponsor approval via Stage‑Gates





This Project Scope Statement is complemented by:

Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
WBS Dictionary
Requirements Specification

Together, these documents define the Scope Baseline.

title: OKR (Monitoring, Control and Value Tracking)
text:
Enable automated screening of AUV imagery through Computer Vision to measurably reduce human effort, analysis time, and operational cost in subsea inspection activities, with initial validation in partnership with NOAA and clear potential for reuse across Petrobras Subsea operations.




The exponential growth of AUV inspection campaigns has resulted in a proportional increase in:

Manual image analysis effort
Specialist workload and exposure
Inspection cycle time
Operational cost per campaign

Without automation, this model does not scale.
This OKR introduces AI-based automated screening of AUV imagery as a foundational digital capability to support Subsea environmental and inspection activities. Also, aligns with Petrobras Subsea strategic goals of:

Operational efficiency
Digitalization of subsea inspections
Reduction of human exposure and specialist overload
Creation of reusable digital assets with long-term value





KR1 – Reduction of Manual Analysis Effort
Achieve at least 30% reduction in the volume of non-ecologically relevant AUV images requiring manual expert analysis for at least one validated field (Viola or Espadarte), allowing specialists to concentrate their effort on benthic-relevant and environmentally significant content.
Explicit Ecological Value

Greater specialist focus on benthic habitats, organisms, and ecological structures
Reduced cognitive overload caused by large volumes of irrelevant imagery

Value and ROI:

Direct reduction in specialist hours
Lower inspection analysis cost per campaign


KR2 – Reduction of Analysis Cycle Time
Reduce by at least 25% the elapsed time between AUV image acquisition and availability of ecologically relevant screening results, enabling earlier environmental interpretation and decision-making by NOAA.
Explicit Ecological Value

Earlier access to environmental insights
Improved support for monitoring, impact assessment, and environmental decision processes

Value and ROI:

Faster decision-making
Improved responsiveness for scientific and operational stakeholders


KR3 – Baseline Model Quality and Reliability
Deliver a baseline Computer Vision screening model meeting agreed minimum performance criteria explicitly defined to protect ecologically relevant data, with clearly documented limitations and assumptions.
Explicit Ecological Value

Reduced risk of incorrect exclusion of benthic-relevant imagery
Transparency regarding environmental limitations of the model

Value and ROI:

Increased confidence in automated screening

Reduced rework and operational risk


KR4 – Reusability and Scalability
Demonstrate applicability of the screening pipeline across different operational contexts by evidencing consistent identification of benthic-relevant imagery across at least two fields (Viola and Espadarte).
Explicit Ecological Value

Consistent ecological interpretation across geographic areas
Reduced environmental bias between inspection campaigns

Value and ROI:

Dilution of development cost across future campaigns
Accelerated deployment in new inspections


KR5 – Creation of a Reusable Digital Asset
Consolidate images, metadata, spatial information (shapes), and model artifacts into a single, auditable corporate environmental data asset, enabling long-term reuse in ecological studies and future AI-driven subsea initiatives.
Explicit Ecological Value

Preservation of environmental knowledge over time
Foundation for comparative ecological studies and long-term monitoring

Value and ROI:

Long-term digital asset creation
Foundation for scalable AI and MLOps in Subsea operations






Continuous interaction with the Product Owner (PO)

Minimum cadence: two checkpoints per week

Alignment with NOAA stakeholders for:

Validation of results
Agreement on success criteria

Systematic documentation of:

Technical decisions
Model assumptions
Limitations and risks to ensure transparency and auditability






This OKR establishes a foundational ecological intelligence capability for Subsea operations, with automated screening acting as a strategic enabler to strengthen environmental stewardship at scale.
As a primary objective, the initiative supports:

Improved ecological understanding of benthic environments, through consistent and scalable identification of benthic-relevant imagery
Higher confidence in environmental baseline studies, reducing the risk of overlooking ecologically relevant features
Enhanced support to environmental monitoring, licensing, and impact assessment activities, under NOAA / SUB responsibility

Building on this ecological foundation, the automated capability further enables, as secondary and progressive outcomes:

Advanced anomaly detection (e.g., changes in benthic patterns, disturbances, or atypical features)
Autonomous and semi-autonomous inspection workflows, reducing manual burden without compromising environmental rigor
Integration with broader digital subsea ecosystems, combining inspection, environmental, and operational data
Progressive adoption of MLOps practices within SUB, ensuring long-term sustainability, traceability, and reuse of environmental AI assets

title: Roadmap do Produto
text:
This Roadmap provides a strategic, time‑phased view of value delivery, showing how the project evolves from concept to a reusable environmental intelligence capability.
It is intended for:

Executive and sponsor visibility
Strategic alignment and expectation management
Decision support at Stage‑Gates

The Roadmap complements (but does not replace) the Schedule Baseline.





Ecological value is delivered first
Automation is introduced incrementally as an enabler
Each phase ends with a clear decision point
No phase advances without ecological validation
MVP is the minimum viable step toward long‑term capability














Phase
Name
Strategic Focus
Primary Outcome




Phase 0
Strategic Foundation
Governance & alignment
Project authorized


Phase 1
Ecological MVP
Safe automation
Ecologically validated MVP


Phase 2
Generalization & Reuse
Consistency & scale
Multi‑field capability


Phase 3
Assetization
Long‑term value
Corporate environmental asset


Phase 4
Future Evolution
(Out of scope)
Advanced capabilities









Objective: Establish governance, scope, and ecological safeguards
Key Activities

Project Charter approval
Business Case approval
Strategy & Governance (OKRs, Value Stream)
Stakeholder Register
Scope Baseline (Scope Statement, WBS, WBS Dictionary)
Requirements and Risk baselined

Value Delivered

Clear mandate
Ecological risk explicitly governed
Alignment across SUB and NOAA

Decision Point

G0 — Strategic Authorization





Objective: Prove that AI can safely support ecological screening
Key Activities

Data ingestion and preparation
Annotation guidelines and pilot labeling
AI model development (binary screening)
Ecological validation on first field (Viola or Espadarte)

Primary Value Delivered

Protection of benthic‑relevant data
Reduction of irrelevant screening workload
Faster access to environmental insights

KPIs Emphasized

KPI‑3: Ecological Data Protection Index
KPI‑1: Ecologically Relevant Focus Ratio
KPI‑2: Environmental Screening Lead Time

Decision Point

G2 — MVP Ecological Validation





Objective: Demonstrate ecological consistency across contexts
Key Activities

Validation on second field
Cross‑field performance analysis
Refinement of model and guidelines
Reuse readiness assessment

Primary Value Delivered

Consistent ecological interpretation
Reduced field‑specific bias
Confidence for broader reuse

KPIs Emphasized

KPI‑4: Cross‑Field Ecological Consistency
KPI‑3: Sustained ecological protection

Decision Point

G3 — Reuse & Operational Value Approval





Objective: Convert project outputs into reusable corporate assets
Key Activities

Dataset and model versioning
Traceability and audit consolidation
Governance handover to data stewardship
Documentation and closure

Primary Value Delivered

Long‑term environmental digital asset
Foundation for future AI initiatives
Reduced marginal cost for new campaigns

KPIs Emphasized

KPI‑5: Reusable Environmental Asset Coverage

Decision Point

G4 — Scaling & Assetization Authorization






This phase is explicitly out of scope but intentionally enabled by the MVP.

Potential Directions

Advanced anomaly detection
Temporal ecological change analysis
Integration with sonar and bathymetry
Semi‑autonomous inspection workflows
Full MLOps for Subsea environmental AI

Governance Note

Requires new Business Case and Charter

title: OKRs → KPIs Mapping
text:
Detailed KPI Definitions


KPI-1 — Ecologically Relevant Focus Ratio
(Primary ecological KPI)
Definition
Percentage of expert-reviewed images that are classified as benthic-relevant after AI screening.
Formula
(Number of benthic-relevant images reviewed by experts)
-------------------------------------------------------
(Total number of images reviewed by experts)
Target (MVP)
≥ 70%
Why Executives Care

Indicates whether expert time is being spent where ecological value exists
Direct proxy for KR1 ecological intent




KPI-2 — Environmental Screening Lead Time
(Primary ecological KPI)
Definition
Elapsed time between AUV image acquisition and availability of ecologically relevant screening results to NOAA.
Target (MVP)
≥ 25% reduction compared to manual baseline
Why Executives Care

Faster environmental insight
Improved responsiveness for monitoring, licensing, and assessments




KPI-3 — Ecological Data Protection Index
(Primary ecological risk-control KPI)
Definition
Retention rate of true benthic-relevant images after automated screening.
Formula
(True benthic-relevant images retained)
--------------------------------------
(Total true benthic-relevant images)
Target (MVP)
≥ 85–90%
Why Executives Care

Directly controls ecological risk
Protects credibility of environmental studies




KPI-4 — Cross-Field Ecological Consistency
(Primary ecological scalability KPI)
Definition
Degree to which screening performance (precision/retention) remains stable across different fields (e.g., Viola vs Espadarte).
Target (MVP)
Variation ≤ agreed tolerance threshold between fields
Why Executives Care

Ensures environmental consistency
Reduces field-specific bias and rework




KPI-5 — Reusable Environmental Asset Coverage
(Strategic, long-term KPI)
Definition
Percentage of screened images and associated metadata consolidated into versioned, auditable corporate repositories.
Target (MVP)
≥ 90% of processed data assets versioned and reusable
Why Executives Care

Confirms creation of long-term environmental digital assets
Enables reuse beyond the initial project




KPI-6 — Expert Effort Reduction
(Secondary, enabling KPI)
Definition
Reduction in total specialist hours required for initial screening compared to manual baseline.
Target (MVP)
≥ 30% reduction
Why Executives Care

Confirms operational efficiency
Supports sustainability of expert workforce




KPI-7 — Analysis Cost per Campaign
(Secondary, enabling KPI)
Definition
Average cost of image screening per AUV campaign.
Target (MVP)
Downward trend demonstrated (absolute value contextual)
Why Executives Care

Demonstrates economic sustainability
Supports scaling decisions

Contingency and fallback actions
Integration with KPIs and Stage‑Gates

Re‑screen affected datasets manually

section: Subsea Benthic Analysis AI – Multi-Modal Environmental Intelligence Pipeline
text: Subsea Benthic Analysis AI – Multi-Modal Environmental Intelligence Pipeline

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Project Identification
Project Name
Subsea Benthic Analysis AI
Sponsor
Núcleo Operacional de Análise Ambiental de Imagens Marinhas - NOAA-IM
Product Owner (PO)
Walace Rosa Laurindo de Oliveira
Project Manager
Designated by SUB
Date
[To be filled]

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Purpose and Justification
The purpose of this project is to establish an AI-enabled environmental intelligence capability to support ecological analysis of subsea environments, with initial focus on automated screening of AUV imagery.
The project addresses a critical scalability challenge: the exponential growth of subsea inspection data has exceeded the capacity of manual ecological analysis, increasing risks related to:

Delayed environmental assessments

Specialist overload

Potential loss or late identification of ecologically relevant benthic information

Automation is introduced as an enabler, not as an end in itself, to preserve and strengthen ecological rigor at scale.
The strategic justification for this initiative is detailed in the Business Case (?@sec-business-case).

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Strategic Alignment
This project is aligned with Petrobras Subsea (SUB) strategic drivers:

Environmental stewardship and ecological responsibility
Digitalization of subsea inspection and monitoring
Reduction of human exposure and specialist overload
Creation of reusable digital environmental assets
Long-term sustainability of environmental analysis capabilities

The project is executed in partnership with NOAA (internal SUB unit), which provides environmental validation authority.

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Objectives

Primary Objective (MVP)
Develop and validate a binary AI-based screening capability capable of distinguishing:

Subsea imagery containing benthic-relevant ecological features
Subsea imagery that is non-ecologically relevant for benthic analysis

The MVP prioritizes precision and ecological protection over aggressive automation.



Secondary Objectives

Reduce manual ecological screening effort while preserving expert authority

Shorten environmental analysis cycles for AUV campaigns

Establish a scalable, offline-first AI pipeline

Create reusable, auditable environmental digital assets

Prepare technical foundations for future detection, segmentation, and ecological intelligence use cases

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
High-Level Scope

In Scope (MVP)

Automated screening of AUV still images

Batch and offline-first processing

AI-assisted annotation with human-in-the-loop validation

Environmental SME oversight and validation

Dataset and model versioning



Out of Scope (MVP)

Real-time or streaming deployment

Full taxonomic classification

Autonomous decision-making without human validation

Advanced multimodal fusion beyond still imagery

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Definition of Success (High-Level)
Project success is defined by ecological value delivery, supported by operational and economic benefits.
High-level success indicators include:

Demonstrated protection of benthic-relevant data

Measurable reduction in manual screening effort

Reduction in time to environmental insight

Positive validation by NOAA / SUB environmental specialists

Creation of reusable environmental data assets

Detailed success metrics are defined in the OKRs → KPIs Executive Tracking document.

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Key Risks (High-Level)

Risk of ecological data loss due to model error

Annotation subjectivity and inconsistency

Class imbalance in subsea imagery

Adoption resistance if ecological confidence is not preserved

Mitigation strategies are defined in the Risk Register (?@sec-risk-register) and governed through KPI-based Stage-Gates (?@sec-stage-gates).

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Governance and Decision Framework
This project follows a Stage-Gate governance model (?@sec-stage-gates), where progression is authorized only if ecological protection KPIs meet minimum thresholds.
Key governance principles:

Environmental protection KPIs take precedence over efficiency KPIs

Human-in-the-loop validation is mandatory

No scaling decision may be taken without ecological validation

The detailed governance model is defined in: - Strategy & Governance – Value Stream & OKRs
- OKRs → KPIs Executive Tracking
- Stage-Gates KPI-Based Governance

title: Termo de Abertura do Projeto (TAP)
text:
Authorization
This Project Charter formally authorizes:

Allocation of resources for Planning and MVP Execution

Engagement of NOAA / SUB environmental specialists

Initiation of AI-assisted ecological screening activities

Approval of this Charter authorizes the project to proceed according to the defined governance framework.
